queries: management of musculoskeletal back pain lower rib cage
NSAID dosing for musculoskeletal back pain

evidence.title: Management of Acute Pain from Non–Low Back Musculoskeletal Injuries: Guidelines from AAFP and ACP | AAFP

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left side lower rib cage back pain that started 3 weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, respiratory symptoms, neurological symptoms, swelling, bruising, or skin changes. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief. There is no history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, abdominal pain, osteoporosis, cancer, or recent steroid use.

O: Phone consultation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain localized to the left lower rib cage without red flag symptoms.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or myofascial pain
2. Costochondritis or rib dysfunction
3. Herpes zoster (early stage without rash)
4. Referred pain from visceral causes (less likely given no systemic symptoms)

P: First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg PO twice daily with food for 7 days
Alternatives: Diclofenac 50 mg PO three times daily with food for 7 days; Meloxicam 15 mg PO once daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal risk factors; advise taking with food to reduce GI irritation; monitor for any signs of allergic reaction or worsening pain
Return precautions: Return if pain worsens, new neurological symptoms develop (numbness, weakness, tingling), fever or systemic symptoms occur, or if no improvement after 7 days of treatment
Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flags and normal symptom profile
